title: Chocolate Self-Saucing Pudding
subtitle: A chocolate sponge that creates its own sauce during baking
excerpt: One batter goes into the tin. A dusting of cocoa and brown sugar goes on top. Boiling water is poured over. During baking, the sponge rises through the liquid and the sauce sinks beneath, giving you a light chocolate sponge sitting over a glossy dark sauce. One dish, no custard required.
sourceNotes: Chocolate self-saucing pudding is a mid-twentieth century recipe found across British and Australian home cooking. The technique of pouring boiling water over a cocoa-dusted batter to create a sauce during baking appears in British home cookery books from the 1950s onward.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten straight from the oven. The sauce absorbs into the sponge as it cools.

content.text: The self-saucing pudding looks improbable the first time you make it: you pour boiling water over uncooked batter and expect something useful to come out of the oven. What happens is that the sponge batter is lighter than the water and rises during baking, while the sugar and cocoa on top dissolve into the water and sink below the sponge to form the sauce. The result is a distinct sponge layer sitting over a pool of chocolate sauce, all in one dish.

Pour the boiling water gently so you don't disturb the cocoa-sugar layer beneath it. The three-layer stack in the tin (batter, then cocoa-sugar dusting, then water) needs to go into the oven as undisturbed as possible. Serve immediately: the sauce absorbs into the sponge as it sits.
A 1.5-litre baking dish or deep roasting tin. Butter it lightly. You will also need 300 ml of boiling water to finish.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Whisk {{self-raising-flour}} of self-raising flour, 30 g of cocoa powder, and {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar together in a large bowl. Add {{unsalted-butter}} of melted butter, {{whole-milk}} of whole milk, the beaten eggs, and {{vanilla-extract}} of vanilla extract. Stir to a smooth, thick batter.
Spread the batter evenly in the buttered dish. Mix {{dark-brown-sugar}} of dark brown sugar with 25 g of cocoa powder and scatter evenly over the batter surface. Do not stir.

Pour 300 ml of boiling water very slowly over the back of a large spoon held just above the cocoa-sugar layer. This breaks the force of the water and keeps the layers intact. Do not stir. Transfer to the oven immediately and bake for 25, 30 minutes until the sponge surface is set and springs back when pressed gently. A skewer inserted into the sponge layer should come out clean; the sauce beneath will still be liquid.

Rest for 5 minutes. Serve directly from the dish in deep bowls, making sure each portion gets sponge and sauce from the bottom. A scoop of vanilla ice cream or a pour of cold cream alongside works well against the warm sauce.

items.symptom: Sponge and sauce are mixed together rather than separated
items.cause: The water was poured too quickly and disturbed the layers, or the batter was too thin
items.fix: Pour the water very slowly over the back of a spoon. Make sure the batter is thick before adding the water, if it looks runny, the flour ratio may be off.

items.symptom: There is very little sauce beneath the sponge
items.cause: Overbaked, or not enough water was added
items.fix: Bake for the minimum time and serve as soon as possible. Check that 300 ml of boiling water was added. The sauce absorbs into the sponge as the pudding sits.

items.cause: Underbaked, or too much water was added
items.fix: Test the sponge surface with a skewer, it should come out clean from the sponge layer. If still wet, return to the oven for 5 minutes. Stick to the measured 300 ml of water.

items.symptom: Sauce is too thick and sticky
items.cause: Overbaked or the dish was too wide so the water evaporated before the sponge rose
items.fix: Use a dish with the dimensions given so the water depth is adequate. Watch the baking time carefully, the sponge should just be set.

content.text: The self-saucing pudding became a British and Australian household classic in the mid-twentieth century, when the technique of pouring liquid over batter before baking began appearing in domestic cookery books. The chocolate version is the most widely made, though lemon self-saucing puddings using the same method are common in Australia. The appeal is practical as much as culinary: one dish, no separate sauce to make, and a result that looks more elaborate than the method warrants.
